title: Bindings, Scope, Closures, and Lifetime · Retrieval Circuit
summary: Build configurable counters and validators without leaking mutable state across community-service widgets. Learners retrieve rules and procedures before seeing feedback. Planned artifact: Extend the evidence record for a closure-backed state and validation library after a delayed changed case. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Choose const, let, or legacy var from reassignment, scope, and initialization behavior.
Resolve names through lexical environments and identify shadowing without guessing from call order.
Predict declaration instantiation, function hoisting, and temporal dead-zone failures.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

starterFiles.javascript:
// javascript-basics cumulative stakeholder program.
// Add one runnable behavior, assertion, changed case, and evidence return per activity.

steps.title: Separate evidence from confidence
steps.instruction:
review case — Create two independent status counters seeded at 6 and 9. Calls must preserve closure isolation, explicit inputs and returns, and a trace showing why a shadowed binding does not update outer state.

Inspect the Garbage Reachability trace for this review case. Which record connects a precondition, observed state change, and boundary result?
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for reason about reachability, event-listener cleanup, weak references, and accidental retention without manual memory control.

content.text: For Garbage Reachability, distinguish a claim from evidence by locating the relevant precondition, trace or invariant, and changed input result.

hints: Name the Garbage Reachability precondition or invariant that the review case must preserve.

Trace this concrete case before choosing: Create two independent status counters seeded at 6 and 9. Calls must preserve closure isolation, explicit inputs and returns, and a trace showing why a shadowed binding does not update outer state.
Use the documented misconception as a counterexample target: The garbage collector removes every object as soon as a function returns.
